steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank sits between two independent clauses: “Compiling isn't exclusive to certain programming languages” and “any language can incorporate this tool.” The contrast word however must be punctuated correctly relative to both clauses.

steps.title: Two competing readings
steps.content:
• Reading 1 (favors A — 'languages; however,'): Treat however as a conjunctive adverb introducing the second clause. The textbook rule is semicolon-before, comma-after: X; however, Y. This is the standard, idiomatic pattern taught in nearly every grammar handbook and is the most common SAT answer shape for this structure.
  • Reading 2 (favors D — 'languages, however;'): Treat however as a trailing parenthetical sentence adverb attached to the end of the first clause — “Compiling isn't exclusive to certain programming languages, however” — with the semicolon then performing the independent-clause join to “any language can incorporate this tool.” Under this reading, the comma sets off the parenthetical however from its host clause and the semicolon does the boundary work between the two independent clauses.

steps.title: Eliminate B and C
steps.content:
• Choice B — 'languages, however,': A comma on each side of however leaves only a comma between two independent clauses, producing a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice C — 'languages. However,': Grammatically legal as two sentences, but the surrounding passage punctuates this as a single sentence and Standard English Conventions items typically have exactly one fully correct answer. ✗

steps.title: Why this item is flagged
steps.content: The official key records D. Choice A reflects the more standard and widely taught pattern for joining two independent clauses with the conjunctive adverb however (semicolon-before, comma-after). Choice D is defensible only if you read however as a trailing parenthetical on the first clause rather than as an introductory conjunctive adverb on the second — a less common but not impossible parsing. Because both readings have grammatical support and reasonable test-prep sources would flag A as the canonical answer, this item is retained as needs_review. On the actual exam, trust the College Board key (D); for general writing, prefer the A pattern.
